coredata.dc:title: Multicolor circularly polarized luminescence from chiral carbon dots and cellulose nanocrystals assemblies for visual polarization detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Wenyan
Lu, Di
Liu, Junxue
Lu, Siyu
Pan, Qinhe
Li, Jiyang
coredata.dc:description: Chiral carbon dots (CCDs) combined with outstanding photoluminescence and unique chirality have become intriguing carbon-based nanomaterials, however, the realization of the optical activity in both ground and excited states remains a great challenge. To overcome this difficulty, multicolor CCDs have been synthesized by changing surface oxidation states and P atom doping via solvothermal route, which emit unusual near-ultraviolet, and blue, yellow and red fluorescence with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) of 28.99%, 22.55%, 84.63%, and 75.73%, respectively. Benefiting from the excellent luminescence of CCDs and chiral environment of cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs), a series of CCDs@CNCs composite films were produced by matrix-assisted method. The electrostatic, H-bonds and vdWs interactions are found to drive the formation of co-assembly and facilitate the chiral transfer and amplify between CNCs and chromophores. Thus, the CCDs@CNCs films display bright and multicolor right-handed circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) with extremely high luminescence dissymmetry factor (g lum) values of −0.24 to −0.90. The synergy of intense g lum and high efficiency of multicolor composite films endow them with a potential for visual polarization detection. This research provides inspiration and feasibility for constructing CPL-active materials in co-assembly system and harvesting high g lum value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Circularly polarized luminescence
Chiral carbon dots
Multicolor
Cellulose nanocrystals
Visual polarization detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and ultrasonic treatment of cellulose nanocrystals
Calculation of the selective reflection wavelength and luminescence dissymmetry factor
Preparation of multicolor chiral carbon dots (CCDs)
Optical and chiral properties of CCDs
Circular polarized luminscence of CCDs@CNCs composite films
Visual detection devices by CCDs@CNCs films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible absorption spectra, FL excitation and emission spectra of (a) LP-PCDs, (b) LP-BCDs, (c) LP-YCDs and (d) LP-RCDs aqueous solutions (Illustration: images under sunlight and 365 nm UV light). Two-dimensional fluorescence spectra of (e) LP-PCDs, (f) LP-BCDs, (g) LP-YCDs and (h) LP-RCDs aqueous solutions. Circular dichroism spectra of (i) LP-PCDs, (j) LP-BCDs, (k) LP-YCDs and (l) LP-RCDs aqueous solutions.

TEM and HRTEM images of (a) LP-PCDs, (b) LP-BCDs, (c) LP-YCDs and (d) LP-RCDs. The size distributions of (e) LP-PCDs, (f) LP-BCDs, (g) LP-YCDs and (h) LP-RCDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of four CCDs (i) C 1 s, (j) N 1 s, (k) O 2 s, (l) P 2p.

(a) Surface potential histograms of 0.2 wt% cellulose and four CCDs (insert from left to right are images of 2 wt% CNCs, LP-PCDs@CNCs, LP-BCDs@CNCs, LP-YCDs@CNCs and LP-RCDs@CNCs suspensions); (b) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra; (c) UV–vis transmission spectra; (d) Circular dichroism spectra; Cross-sectional SEM and Polarized optical microscopy images of (e, i) LP-PCDs@CNCs, (f, j) LP-BCDs@CNCs, (g, k) LP-YCDs@CNCs and (h, l) LP-RCDs@CNCs composite films.

(a) CPL spectra and (b) luminescence dissymmetry factor curves of LP-PCDs@CNC, LP-BCDs@CNC, LP-YCDs@CNC, and LP-RCDs@CNC; (c) A comparison of the luminescence dissymmetry factors of reported CDs- or CNC-based CPL materials with four composite films. (d) A schematic diagram of the LP-RCDs@CNC composite film for visual polarization detection.

Fluorescence images of multicolor composite films under the conditions of left-handed (upper layer) and right-handed polarizer (lower layer) (λex = 365 nm) (a) LP-PCDs@CNCs; (b) LP-BCDs@CNCs; (c) LP-YCDs@CNCs and (d) LP-RCDs@CNCs; Fluorescence emission spectra of multicolor composite films with different rotating circular polarizers (e) LP-PCDs@CNCs; (f) LP-BCDs@CNCs; (g) LP-YCDs@CNCs and (h) LP-RCDs@CNCs.
The strategy diagram for syntheses of CCDs and the co-assembly of CCDs and CNCs to construct circularly polarized fluorescence materials.

head.ce:title: Multicolor circularly polarized luminescence from chiral carbon dots and cellulose nanocrystals assemblies for visual polarization detection

School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and College of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials of Tropical Island Resources, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology and College of Science, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral carbon dots (CCDs) combined with outstanding photoluminescence and unique chirality have become intriguing carbon-based nanomaterials, however, the realization of the optical activity in both ground and excited states remains a great challenge. To overcome this difficulty, multicolor CCDs have been synthesized by changing surface oxidation states and P atom doping via solvothermal route, which emit unusual near-ultraviolet, and blue, yellow and red fluorescence with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) of 28.99%, 22.55%, 84.63%, and 75.73%, respectively. Benefiting from the excellent luminescence of CCDs and chiral environment of cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs), a series of CCDs@CNCs composite films were produced by matrix-assisted method. The electrostatic, H-bonds and vdWs interactions are found to drive the formation of co-assembly and facilitate the chiral transfer and amplify between CNCs and chromophores. Thus, the CCDs@CNCs films display bright and multicolor right-handed circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) with extremely high luminescence dissymmetry factor (g lum) values of −0.24 to −0.90. The synergy of intense g lum and high efficiency of multicolor composite films endow them with a potential for visual polarization detection. This research provides inspiration and feasibility for constructing CPL-active materials in co-assembly system and harvesting high g lum value.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is a unique characteristic in nature and biological systems, which has been extensively studied from both fundamental research and application viewpoints [1,2]. Chiral materials exhibiting circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) have received great attention because of the importance of circular polarization in the essential photon effects in biological and chemical information carriers and optoelectronic devices [3–5]. CPL is based on the chirality and fluorescence of the material, which can reflect the structural information of chiral luminescent materials in the excited state [6,7]. CPL-active materials have promising applications in 3D displays [8], asymmetric catalytic systems [9], information encryption and storage [10,11], probes [12], and enantiomeric detection [13,14]. The design and synthesis of CPL materials have emerged as a hot research area in the field of chiral luminescent materials [15].

In recent years, chiral carbon dots (CCDs) stand out among many chiral luminescent materials and serve as promising candidates to construct CPL-active materials due to their extraordinary advantages of simple synthesis, biological friendliness, excellent photoluminescence, and good solubility [16]. Usually, CCDs are prepared starting from chiral precursors, and their bandgap fluorescence emissions can be regulated by the design of the precursors and control of the reaction condition [17]. Apart from the commonly observed blue-emitting CCDs, continuous efforts have been devoted to the preparation of multicolor CCDs with high PLQY. CCDs over the entire visible range (blue to red) were reported in 2021 for the first time, and the PLQY of red-emitting CCDs is 53.62 % [18]. Regrettably, bandgap-tunable CCDs emitting at a shorter wavelength than blue light, particularly in the ultraviolet range, are scarcely rare. In particular, the majority of various CCDs are only optically active in the ground state giving circular dichroism while not excited-state chirality, minority are CPL-active but the major drawback is the low dissymmetry factor (g lum), usually on the order of 10-4 [19–21]. Therefore, the exploration of multicolor CCDs extending from ultraviolet to visible range and amplify the g lum value are of upmost important for CDs-based CPL materials.

Given the host–guest chemistry, it is a reasonable approach to introduce a strong chiral environment to improve the chirality of excited states [22,23], in addition to the inherent chirality of CCDs. As a result, a co-assembly strategy is ideal to build efficient CDs-based CPL materials [24,25]. For examples, the CDs-composed G-quartet hydrogels with CPL activity (|g lum| = 0.12) were generated by using a one-step microwave-assisted procedure [26]. C-dots/chiral helical polymer bilayer composite film exhibit intense CPL (|g lum| = 0.14) and circularly polarized room temperature phosphorescence (|g lum| = 0.012) [27]. Among the numerous chiral matrix candidates, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) as typical chiral polymers possess solute-type liquid crystal quality [28], which would produce a photonic crystal film with a left-handed chiral nematic structure via evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) [29], providing a strong chiral assembly environment for CDs to achieve the high glum value of excited-state chirality.

Herein, we designed and synthesized CCDs with tunable fluorescence from near-ultraviolet to red using a solvothermal approach starting from the precursors of nitrogen-containing L-proline and o-phenylenediamine with adjusted solvent ratio, acidity, and heteroatom doping. In comparison to previously reported CCDs, the near-ultraviolet-emitting CCDs with an emission peak at 380 nm (PLQY: 28.99 %) are first reported, and the PLQY of red-emitting CCDs has reached the higher value of 75.73 %. Moreover, the multicolor CCDs are successfully co-assembled with CNCs with specific photonic forbidden bandgaps by a convenient and effective host–guest co-assembly, emitting right-handed CPL. The prepared composite films are not only easy to produce and repeatable, but also have excellent photostability and intense g lum (highest value is of −0.90), which are expected to have the potential for visualization of circular polarization detection, information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

All chemicals are used without further purification otherwise indicated. L-proline (C5H9NO2, ≥99 %) was purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. O-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2, ≥98.5 %) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %) were bought from Sinophram Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethanol absolute (C2H6O) and orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4) were supplied by Tianjin Jindongtianzheng Precision Chemical Reagent Factory. The cotton pulp board was purchased from Hebei Paper Group of China. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Industry Group Co., Ltd. Potassium bromide (KBr) was provided by Tianjin guangfu technology development Co., Ltd. Milli-Q deionized water was used in all experiments.

The transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) and the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100F scanning transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum was measured on a Bruker VERTEX 80/80v with KBr pellets, and a baseline correction was applied after measurement. The chemical composition was conducted by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements using ThermoFisher Scientific EscaLab 250 Xi. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained on a JSM-7800F (JEOL Ltd). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were obtained using a Scientific FluoroMax-4 spectra fluorometer (Horiba Jobin-Yvon). The FL lifetime (with TCPSC Technology) was collected on an Edinburgh FLS920. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was recorded on an FLS920 with an integrating sphere. UV–visible absorption spectra (UV–vis) were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer using a quartz cell with a 1 cm optical path. Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were analyzed in automatic mode using a BioLogic MOS-450 spectra polarimeter. Circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) spectra were recorded on a JASCO CPL-300. Polarized optical microscopy (POM) images of films were measured on Leica DM4000M Versatile Upright Microscope. The physical images and supporting movies were taken using an iPhone 11 under UV light.

0.1 g L-proline (L-Pro) and 60 mg O-phenylenediamine (O-PD) were mixed in 5 mL of deionized water until completely dissolved. Then 4.0 mL anhydrous ethanol was added to stir for 15 min, followed by 1 mL phosphoric acid to stir for 30 min. Finally, the reaction solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and reacted for 12 h at 180 °C to produce LP-PCDs stock solution

Weighing 0.1 g L-Pro and 60 mg O-PD into a beaker containing 9.5 mL deionized water. Then, 0.5 mL of H3PO4 was added and agitated for 30 min, and the formed solution was reacted for 12 h at 180 °C to produce LP-BCDs solution
Weighing 0.1 g L-Pro and 60 mg O-PD into a beaker with 10 mL deionized water. The entire system was reacted at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain LP-YCDs solution

0.1 g L-Pro and 60 mg O-PD were added into a 25 mL glass beaker with 9 mL deionized water, then 1 mL phosphoric acid was added and stirred for 30 min. The reaction system was reacted for 12 h at 160 °C to produce LP-RCDs solution

The above prepared CCDs stock solution was purified by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min), and the recovered supernatant was filtered through an aqueous filter (0.22 μm) and dialyzed for 24 h in a deionized water dialysis bag

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and ultrasonic treatment of cellulose nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of cellulose nanocrystals was according to the literature [30]. In detail, 20 g of bleached cotton pulp was hydrolyzed in H2SO4 (64 wt%) at 50 °C for 90 min before being stopped with 10 times as much deionized water. After 24 h, the milky white suspension at the bottom was centrifuged and washed three times (8000 rpm, 6 mins). Then white precipitate was dialyzed until the pH value was stabilized at 5 (MW: 3000 kDa). The ultrasonic probe was submerged in each of four groups of 2.5 mL of 2.0 wt% cellulose suspension. A variety of ultrasonic energies were used to treat the cellulose suspension (machine model: Sonics VCX750, 30 % amplitude, halt for 25 s every 5 s). Four CNCs suspensions named as CNCs0, CNCs8, CNCs22, and CNCs32 were produced with an energy input of 0, 8, 22, and 32 J, respectively

2.5 mL of CNC0, CNCs8, CNCs22, and CNCs32 suspensions were mixed with 100 L of LP-PCDs, LP-BCDs, LP-YCDs, and LP-RCDs solutions, respectively. After stirring at room temperature for 1 h, the mixed mixture was put into a surface dish (35 mm in diameter) and left at room temperature and 30 % humidity to produce evaporation-induced self-assembly. Finally, the multi-color composite films named as LP-PCDs@CNCs, LP-BCDs@CNCs, LP-YCDs@CNCs, and LP-RCDs@CNCs were obtained

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the selective reflection wavelength and luminescence dissymmetry factor
ce:section.ce:para:
When a photonic crystal receives a light wave of a certain wavelength at a specific angle, the derived formula is calculated using Bragg's equation of diffraction as follows Eq.
(1) :
(1)
λ = n P sin θ
According to the test parameters in the UV–vis transmission spectrum and the average refractive index of CNCs, the average refractive index n of the photonic crystal is set to 1.555, and the angle between the incident light and the photonic crystal axis is set to 90°.

The luminescence dissymmetry factor (g lum) is one of the critical metrics for assessing circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) activity, and its calculation formula is as follows Eq.
(2) :
(2)
g lum
=
Δ I
1 2
I
= 2
(
I L
-
I R
)
(
I L
+
I R
)
Here, I L and I R represent the intensities of left- and right-handed circularly polarized luminescence (CPL), respectively, and the maximum theoretical value of |g lum| is 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of multicolor chiral carbon dots (CCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the multicolor CCDs, the design and selection of chiral carbon-containing sources with particular structures are crucial. L-proline is a common chiral amino acid, its high melting point, excellent water solubility, and nitrogen-containing 5-ring facilitate the chiral source to fully react and preserve some chiral structural features on the surface of produced CDs even at high temperature. O-phenylenediamine is often utilized to control the fluorescence emission of CDs due to their sp2 hybrid conjugated structure. The amount of phosphoric acid doping, the solvent composition, and the reaction temperature are varied in an effort to further regulate the fluorescence emission of CCDs. As a result, four varieties of CCDs, known as LP-PCDs, LP-BCDs, LP-YCDs, and LP-RCDs, were successfully synthesized (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and chiral properties of CCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 1
a-d, the aqueous solutions of LP-PCDs, LP-BCDs, LP-YCDs, and LP-RCDs exhibit varying colors in the daylight and corresponding fluorescence in the colors of near-ultraviolet, blue, yellow, and red under UV irradiation, respectively. Their average fluorescence lifetimes are 8.61 ns, 11.90 ns, 2.66 ns, and 1.92 ns ( Fig. S1
,
Table S1 ). At present, CCDs with tunable fluorescence from blue to red have been achieved, however, this is the first report on CCDs with near-ultraviolet fluorescence, and CIE coordinate of LP-PCDs is (0.427, 0.519) ( Fig. S2 ). All of multicolor CCDs display excitation-independent behavior, their maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths (λmax = 380, 450, 560, and 620 nm) stay constant with varying excitation wavelengths, demonstrating that these CCDs possess the relatively uniform and stable chemical state and emission center (Fig. 1 e-h). Notably, among these CCDs, LP-RCDs have the narrowest full width at half maxima (FWHM) of 29.75 nm, and its absolute PLQYs is high up to 75.73 %, much higher than the known reported value ( Table S2 ). UV–vis absorption spectra of multicolor CCDs show different absorption bands in the visible range, which are attributed to the n-π* transition of C = O chromophore [31]. It is interesting to find that the fluorescence excitation peak of CCDs is overlapped with the UV–vis absorption band, and the shift of such absorption peaks is positive correlation with the gradually redshifted fluorescence emission peaks, indicating that their multicolor fluorescence emission are closely interrelated with these absorption bands caused by the relevant surface structures [32].

The detail structure characterizations are performed to investigate the reason for the different fluorescence emission of CCDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show that these CCDs have similar spherical shapes with average sizes of 7.6 nm (LP-PCDs), 8.6 nm (LP-BCDs), 7.9 nm (LP-YCDs), and 6.5 nm (LP- RCDs) (Fig. 2
a-h). The comparable graphitized carbon core structures are discovered, with typical lattice fringes of a spacing of 0.21 nm or 0.28 nm [37,38].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in Fig. S5 indicate that C = O absorption peak at 1732 cm−1 shows a slightly improved trend with their redshift of fluorescence emissions. The stretching vibrations generated by P-O-C, P-C, and P-O bonds are observed for three kinds of CCDs [39,40]. Due to the absence of P-doping, LP-YCDs exhibit significantly different absorption peaks, which have a somewhat larger and stronger absorption bands near 3431 cm−1 (O-H/N-H bonds) and 2979 cm−1 (C-H bonds), and the strong absorption peak at 1628 cm−1 (C = O groups) shifts to lower wavenumber. Full-scan XPS spectra confirm the successful insertion of P, C, N and O elements in LP-PCDs, LP-BCDs and LP-RCDs, while LP-YCDs are absence of P elements ( Fig. S6 ). Compared to other CCDs, the C and N content of LP-YCDs is higher, but the O concentration is lower. Following that, as shown in Fig. 2 i- l and
Table S3 , XPS peak fitting was utilized to accurately discriminate and quantify different elemental chemical states in CCDs. Obviously, the relative contents of O-C = O bonds, P-N/P-C bonds, C-N-C bonds and sp2 hybrid conjugated structure rise gradually, from LP-PCDs, LP-BCDs to LP-RCDs. Thus, the increase of surface oxidation and graphitization degree of CCDs [41], and the new energy level introduced by P doping narrow the band gap result in the redshift of fluorescence emissions of these three CCDs. As for LP-YCDs without P atoms, the proportion of O-C = O groups and nitrogen doping amount, and the sp2 hybrid conjugated carbon cores are all much larger than those of other CCDs, which lead to the yellow-emitting in the long wavelength band [42]. It is noted that these CCDs not only possess optical activity but also present multicolor fluorescence. The key factor in the syntheses of CCDs is the pre-design and selection of CDs precursors invoving a chiral amino acid of L-Pro used to introduce chiral structural features into CDs, and O-PD used to control the fluorescence emission of CDs by adjusting the amount of phosphoric acid, the solvent, and the reaction temperature. When L-tyrosine and para-phenylenediamine were selected as the precursors, the prepared CCDs showed variable fluorescence emissions from 450 nm to 610 nm, as well as a negative Cotton effect. However, their poor water solubility is not beneficial for the subsequent uniform co-assembly with well-soluble CNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Circular polarized luminscence of CCDs@CNCs composite films
ce:section.ce:para:
As a strong chiral substrate, CNCs with left-handed nematic structure have been utilized to facilitate CPL of multicolor CCDs [28,29]. As shown in TEM image ( Fig. S7 ), the morphology of CNCs is a spindle-shaped rod, with a length range of 50–200 nm and a width of tens of nanometers [43]. After mixing CCDs, the CNCs suspensions can still keep transparent and show the characteristic color of CCDs solution (the inset in Fig. 3
a), indicating the uniform dispersion of multicolor CCDs in CNCs suspension. According to the Zeta potential data in Fig. 3 a, electrostatic interaction exists between the positively charged multicolor CCDs (3.79 mV, 14.5 mV, 1.46 mV, and 1.59 mV) and the negatively charged CNCs suspension (–23.9 mV). Also, hydrogen bonds and van der Waals (vdW) forces are existed between CCDs and CNCs since they contain rich surface functional groups (such as hydroxyl groups) [44]. Due to these host–guest interactions, during the process of evaporation-induced self-assembly of CNCs, CCDs will adhere to the surface of CNCs particles and co-assemble with them, then finally uniformly distribute in the chiral nematic structure. As a result, the composite films exhibit distinct angle-dependent structural colors under sunlight, and emit multicolor when exposed to UV light ( Fig. S8
). The maximum fluorescence emissions of composite films are nearly consistent with multicolor CCDs (Fig. 3 b), and the average fluorescence durations are 6.05 ns, 3.78 ns, 3.14 ns, and 2.46 ns for LP-PCDs@CNCs, LP-BCDs@CNCs, LP-YCDs@CNCs, and LP-RCDs@CNCs ( Fig. S9
,
Table S4 ), respectively. Compared to CCDs, the PLQYs of composite films are decreased to 19.60 %, 13.30 %, 56.10 % and 31.12 % ( Fig. S10 and Table S5 ), which can be attributed to the fact that part of the leptonic light emission is restricted by the photonic confinement band of the cellulose film and the aggregation of CCDs within the film. Notably, the abundant hydroxyl functional groups on the CNC surface provide a polar environment akin to that of an aqueous solution for the multicolored CCDs, which facilitates the relatively uniform dispersion of CCDs and avoids the aggregation in some degree within the solid film.

The luminescence asymmetry factor (g lum) is a crucial parameter to evaluate the luminescence purity of CPL materials (Eq. (1)). To achieve the strongest CPL signal and higher g lum, a series of CNCs suspensions are regulated by varying the ultrasonic energy (0 J, 8 J, 22 J, and 32 J) to ensure the largest overlap of photonic bandgap with the emissions of CCDs [44–46]. By increasing ultrasonic energy, the photonic bandgap is optimized to 370 nm, 480 nm, 555 nm, and 620 nm for LP-PCDs@CNCs, LP-BCDs@CNCs, LP-YCDs@CNCs and LP-RCDs@CNCs, respectively ( Fig. 3 c). Additionally, the characteristic peaks of the π-π* electronic transition from CCDs might be observed in the 200–300 nm wavelength region. As shown in Fig. 3 d, CCDs@CNC films exhibit a positive strong cotton effect in the ultraviolet–visible wavelength range, demonstrating the left-handed chiral nematic structure. The major circular dichroism signals come from the chiral nematic liquid crystal structure of CNCs following the EISA procedure, which is in agreement with the photonic bandgap in UV–vis transmission spectra. Besides, two relatively weaker signals in the range of 200 nm ∼ 400 nm are displayed by the films ( Fig. S11 ). Compared to the positive signals of the multicolor CCDs (Fig. 1 i- l), both the peak intensity and shape are markedly enhanced and broadened. This is because that the chiral effect of cellulose films can act on the randomly distributed CCDs during the co-assembly process, which can induce and amplify the chirality of CCDs, resulting in the emergence of new circular dichroism signals.

The cross-section of multicolor CCDs@CNC films are measured using SEM. As shown in Fig. 3 e-h, the layered ordered structure can be easily observed at a size of 1 μm, implying the corresponding left-handed chiral nematic structure. The pitch (P) of LP-PCDs@CNC, LP-BCDs@CNC, LP-YCDs@CNC, and LP-RCDs@CNC is 0.25 μm, 0.31 μm, 0.37 μm, and 0.41 μm, respectively. According to the Bragg formula Eq.
(2) , the selective reflection wavelengths (λ) of these composite films is calculated to be 375 nm, 465 nm, 555 nm, and 615 nm, which are consistent with the measured photonic bandgap values. The polarized optical microscopy (POM) images exhibit beautiful, multi-domain colors from purple to red for these films (Fig. 3 i-l). Meanwhile, the magnified POM pictures show fingerprint-like features, which correlate to the characteristic texture of chiral nematic liquid crystals. Strikingly, different from pure CCDs with only ground-state chirality ( Fig. S4 ), the assemblies promote the excited state chirality and significantly increase g lum values, thus all composite films exhibit negative CPL signals with intense g lum values of −0.24, −0.27, −0.58, and −0.90 ( Fig. 4
a, b). Best to our knowledge, the g lum value of LP-RCDs@CNCs with a maximum of −0.90 is highest among the reported CDs-based or CNCs-based CPL materials (Fig. 4 c,
Table S6 ) [26,27,43,44,47–50]. Such high g lum of the composite films can be attributed to the strong chirality of chiral nematic structure of CNCs films, intense non-covalent interactions of guest–host, as well as the large overlap between the photonic bandgap of the composite film and the fluorescence emission of multicolor CCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection devices by CCDs@CNCs films
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the strong CPL capabilities of CCDs@CNCs films, a simple and visual detection device is built that can be utilized for polarization information encryption and optical anti-counterfeiting (Fig. 4 d). Left-handed or right-handed circular polarized filter (LCPF or RCPF) are put in front of the test equipment camera while the film is excited by 365 nm UV light. The fluorescence emission phenomena of the films when equipping with different rotating circular polarizers can be observed and recorded in real time by naked eyes. As illustrated in Fig. 5
a-d, when the LCPF is used, the composite films emit multicolor fluorescence with low intensity. On the contrary, the multicolor composite film becomes brighter and the luminescence intensity is also significantly enhanced when employing the RCPF. To eliminate the differences observed by the naked eye, the fluorescence emission spectra are characterized by using a fluorescence spectrometer with polarizers of different rotation directions. As shown in Fig. 5 e-h, under the corresponding optimal excitation wavelength, LP-PCDs@CNCs, LP-BCDs@CNCs, LP-YCDs@CNCs, and LP-RCDs@CNCs with LCPF and RCPF show the maximum fluorescence emissions at 411 nm, 450 nm, 550 nm and 620 nm, respectively. Obviously, these CCDs@CNCs films display stronger fluorescence under the condition of a RCPF than LCPF. The above light phenomenon has the potential in the application of chiroptical anticounterfeiting.

In this work, CCDs with multicolor fluorescence emissions from near-ultraviolet to red, stable circular dichroism signals and high PLQYs up to 84.63 % have been prepared by using a solvothermal method through controlling the precursors and reaction conditions. According to the study of structures and compositions of CCDs, on the one hand, N/P doping can alter the electronic structure of CCDs by introducing new energy levels, on the other hand, the expansion of the sp2 hybrid conjugated domain and the degree of surface oxidation lead the narrow of energy band of CCDs, the of above elements combine to cause a redshift in the fluorescence emissions. Even without P element doping, LP-YCDs can exhibit yellow fluorescence due to the more O-C = O groups, nitrogen-containing groups, and larger sp2 hybrid conjugated domain. Furthermore, the CCDs@CNCs composite films capable of emitting multicolor right-handed CPL are created via EISA by integrating the strong-emission CCDs with the chiral soft-template CNCs through intense non-covalent interactions of guest–host. By regulating the ultrasound energy, the overlap of photon forbidden band location of the composite film and the emission peak position of CCDs is maximized to get a greater luminescence dissymmetry factor, and the maximum |g lum| value reaches up to 0.90. Based on the left-handed chiral nematic structure and tunable fluorescence, the composite film realizes visual polarization difference under the condition of configuring circular polarizers with different rotation directions, which provides the possibility for CPL materials in the application of information encryption, optical anti-counterfeiting and polarization detection.

Wenyan Ma: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Di Lu: Formal analysis. Junxue Liu: Formal analysis. Siyu Lu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Qinhe Pan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Funding acquisition. Jiyang Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the 111 Project (B17020), the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan (20230101026JC) and the Specific Research Fund of the Innovation Platform for Academicians of Hainan Province, China (YSPTZX202321) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.153453.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
